items.q: Are the continents fixed in place?
items.correct: No — tectonic plates move a few centimetres a year, driven by mantle convection, slab pull and ridge push, slowly rearranging the oceans and continents.
items.misconception: The map of the continents has always looked the way it does now.
items.why: Plate movement underlies every landform and hazard in The Restless Earth.

items.q: Do volcanoes only occur at plate boundaries?
items.correct: Mostly they do, but hot spots like Hawaii form volcanoes in plate interiors where plumes of hot mantle rise, so the boundary rule has important exceptions.
items.misconception: Every volcano sits exactly on a plate margin.
items.why: Hot spots test whether students understand mechanisms rather than memorised maps.

items.q: Does a bigger earthquake always cause more deaths?
items.correct: No — impacts depend on depth, distance from population, building quality, preparedness and response; moderate quakes in vulnerable places can kill far more than great quakes in prepared ones.
items.misconception: Magnitude alone determines an earthquake's death toll.
items.random_wrong: Earthquakes are harmless if they happen at weekends.
items.why: Comparing rich and poor country case studies hinges on vulnerability, not just magnitude.
The answer examiners want is: Richter. “Mercalli” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The San Andreas Fault is what type of margin?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Conservative. “Constructive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was the magnitude of the 2010 Haiti earthquake?
items.why: The real figure is 7 Richter. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
